module: Module 2: In Search of a Universal Theory
content: So in the previous subsection we looked at Marx's ideas of historical materialism and base and superstructure where he explains how capitalism came about. Now in this next subsection, we will discuss his Marx's ideas about how he thinks that these forces that have been unleashed by capitalism will lead to its own downfall, and Marx believes that the next stage will be communism. Of course, we know that communism really failed but this is Marx making the claim that communism will succeed. So how is it, why is Marx saying that now, you know, from feudal we went to the Bourgeoisie society and now he's saying that now from this bourgeoisie society, inevitably we will have communism. Why is he saying that? He is saying that modern bourgeoisie society is like a saucer who is no longer able to control the powers of the netherworld whom he has called up by his spells. So Marx is saying that these forces have been unleashed and now capitalism can no longer control these forces and those same forces will destroy capitalism. So, how is it that this destruction will come about? One, according to Marx, it's because of these economic crisis. So there are periodically economies go through these cycles and there are these crisis happen when there is this epidemic of overproduction, which threatens the entire bourgeoisie society. So too much overproduction, there is not enough demand, markets are shrinking, and then everything starts to shrink and the economy basically goes into a downturn. Marx says, how do bourgeoisie overcome these crisis? Through several ways. They enforce destruction of productive forces so they actually go and destroy products or the conquest of new markets, or the more thorough exploitation of old ones. Now in addition, so these crisis are one way in which, according to Marx, capitalism is creating its own end. The other even more important way, according to Marx, is that the bourgeoisie, the capitalism has given rise to this class called the proletariat, and the proletariat will now destroy the capitalist system. So he says that the weapons used to overthrow feudalism will now be turned against the bourgeoisie itself. But not only has the bourgeoisie forced the weapons that bring debt to itself, it has also called into existence, the men are to wield those weapons, the modern working class, the proletariats. So according to Marx, the bourgeoisie has created its own grave diggers that's what he calls them, grave diggers, who are the proletarians. Now, how does this happen? This is what Marx explains, in proportion as the bourgeoisie that is capital is developed in the same proportion is the proletariat, the modern working class developed. So who are the modern proletariat? A class of laborers who live only as long as they find work and who find work only as long as their labor increases capital. These laborers must sell themselves piecemeal. They're like a commodity, like every other article of commerce, and are consequently exposed to all the vicissitudes of competition, to all the fluctuations of the market. So the proletariats have become a commodity subject to the fluctuations of the market. And now due to machinery and division of labor, their work has lost all charm. They become an appendage of the system, and it is only the most simple, most monotonous, most easily acquired knack that is required of him. This is another quote from Marx. So, the cost of production of a workman is reduced to the bare minimum needs for subsistence. So that's the wage paid, is the bare minimum that you need to survive. The working class is recruited from all the classes of the population, including the previous lower middle class, the small shopkeeper, the small owner, all of these cannot compete with the large industrialists. They just don't have the capital. And so they all become the proletariat, the working class, and as their situation becomes worse and worse. The proletariat starts struggling. They start with individual uncoordinated attacks. They go and attack machinery or they foreign imports which are competing with local products. They go and set fire to them, or they set fire to factories because they are so angry with the capitalists. And so this is, you know, here and there you have these kind of, these uncoordinated attacks. Now if any kind of organization happens, it's actually because of the bourgeoisie who initially seek the help of the proletariat the help of the workers, first in the struggle against the aristocracy, then against the small bourgeoisie, then with the bourgeoisie of foreign countries. And so the bourgeoisie actually help in sort of politicizing these workers. But as the industry develops, the proletariat not only increases in number, it also becomes more concentrated. And as the conditions of life of the proletariat become more and more become worse and worse the conditions also become more similar, so whichever country you go to, wage laborers are having the same struggles, and so the introduction of the machinery destroys these distinction be between the workers. Because now they're all like commodities, there's no, you know, I am a very specialized worker in this sort of specialized worker in this, so they're all somewhat similar and replaceable. And so their wage is also similarly low. And as this capitalism keeps progressing, the lives of the worker become more and more precarious with economic crisis leading to fluctuations. So all of this leads to workers forming trade unions. First, it is for specific demands like keeping a constant wage for more safe working conditions. But later, as the similarity becomes more and more with other trade unions across the world, they become more permanent organizing bodies. So with the growing concentration and size of workers and the improving methods of communication, the Union of Workers grows from uncoordinated local groups to more and more regional and then national, and then pan national groups. And so the whole world takes on the character of a collision between just two classes, the Proletariat and the Bourg Joie. And so, you know, what about these other classes? These small traders? Small owners, landowners? So according to Marx, they all will just collapse into the proletariat and because to Marx, the proletariat is the only really revolutionary class. Other classes such as the small manufacturer, the small shopkeeper, the artisan, the farmer they might fight against the bourgeoisie here and there to protect themselves from extinction, but they're not really revolutionary. They don't want a new system. They just want to roll back the wheels of history and go back to the previous system. So the proletariat, according to Marx, has nothing left. They are just living by working day to day, and so they have nothing to lose. Their mission is to destroy all previous securities for and insurances of individual property, so that according to Marx is the objective. So Marx explains all previous historical movements where movements of minorities or in the interest of minorities. The proletariat movement is an independent self-conscious movement of the immense majority in the interest of the immense majority. The proletariat, the lowest stratum of our present society cannot stir, cannot raise itself up without the whole super incumbent strata of official society being sprung into the air and go . So this is why every form of society has been based on some form of oppression, but the oppressed were at least offered the right to continue to exist, and they had some hope of improvement. So the farmer could raise himself up to be in one of those free communes. But in modern industry, according to Marx situation of the workers just becoming worse and worse, there's no chance of improvement. And so how can you have a system how can you let the bourgeoisie rule they're unfit according to Marx to be the ruling class because they cannot guarantee an existence for their own slave, for their own oppressed class. The I irony according to Marx is that capital is, which is essential for modern industry, is based on labor capital, according to Marx, is accumulated labor. It's the surplus from accumulated labor. And who is it, that was producing who's producing this labor? The workers, the proletariat. Why is wage labor so low? Because of competition between workers. But what is modern industry doing? It is bringing workers together and so modern industry produces its own grave diggers. So according to Marx, the fall of modern industry and the victory of the proletariat is inevitable. So all of these ideas about communism as an ideology is interesting. And, you know, we can have dinner table conversations and debates about it. As social theorists, what we need to take away is the idea that what are the economic relations, what are the different classes that we have in any society and are the classes structured such that everybody is getting a sense of hope that there is improvement. Everybody is doing well, because if not, then at some point people will aggregate and there will be some kind of revolutionary change.
